sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with difficulty swallowing that has been ongoing for approximately four to five weeks.
He describes a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which is more pronounced when eating solids, and occasionally painful.
He has never fully choked but has thought about potentially choking.
The patient's past medical history includes diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently taking metformin 500 milligrams twice a day for diabetes.
He reports that his blood sugars are within the target range when checked, but admits to sometimes skipping blood sugar checks.
He is also taking norvasc for his high blood pressure, with a blood pressure reading of 120/50 mmHg in the office.
The patient denies any nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or other side effects from his medications.
He has not noticed any weight loss, blood in his stool, or vomiting blood.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any coughing, shortness of breath, chest pain, or nausea.
Current medications:
Metformin 500 milligrams twice a day for diabetes; Norvasc for high blood pressure.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 120/50 mmHg.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling or masses.
The thyroid feels normal, and there are no lymph nodes.
The throat exam is normal, with no masses or swelling.
The lung exam is clear, and the heart exam is normal with no murmurs.
The patient has epigastric tenderness in the right upper quadrant, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity exam is fine.
The neurological exam is normal.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has difficulty swallowing, possibly due to a narrowing of the esophagus, which may require a stricture.
The patient is also being followed for diabetes and high blood pressure.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing; Diabetes; Hypertension;
Plan:
I will refer the patient to gastroenterology for a scope to evaluate the difficulty swallowing.
I will also start the patient on prilosec 20 milligrams once a day for reflux.
I recommend that the patient chew their foods frequently, especially solids like steak or meat, to prevent them from getting stuck.
I will also order a hemoglobin A1c and some additional blood work to check kidney function.
I will refer the patient to ophthalmology to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I will continue the patient on metformin and norvasc as currently prescribed.
I will have the patient return in a month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in a month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments.
